CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions are stated early (comparison of delexicalized multi-source transfer vs. a minimally supervised UDP with POS-based priors across 32 UD v1.2 languages). Notation and methodological description appear consistent (delexicalization procedure, concatenation protocol, and hyperparameters are reported), and figures/tables comparing to McDonald et al. (2011) and Rosa (2015) effectively support claims. I deduct one point because presentation lacks a clear theoretical justification for the 10,000-token truncation and the choice/sensitivity of the handcrafted priors is not systematically motivated or ablated in the write-up, which reduces clarity for readers attuned to principled assumptions. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and readable: intro, methods (delexicalized transfer and minimally supervised UDP with priors), experiments on 32 UD v1.2 treebanks, and conclusions follow a logical flow. The main contributions are stated early and supported by thorough multilingual experiments, hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is thorough. Figures/tables appear effective for comparing delexicalized vs minimally supervised results, though the omission of code availability and limited discussion of the 10,000-token training choice slightly weakens reproducibility clarity. Notation and writing are largely consistent and grammatical, with only minor places that could be tightened for readers focused on low-resource deployment details.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions are stated early (comparison of delexicalized multi-source transfer vs. a minimally supervised UDP with POS-based priors across 32 UD v1.2 languages). Notation and methodological description appear consistent (delexicalization procedure, concatenation protocol, and hyperparameters are reported), and figures/tables comparing to McDonald et al. (2011) and Rosa (2015) effectively support claims. I deduct one point because presentation lacks a clear theoretical justification for the 10,000-token truncation and the choice/sensitivity of the handcrafted priors is not systematically motivated or ablated in the write-up, which reduces clarity for readers attuned to principled assumptions.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and readable: intro, methods (delexicalized transfer and minimally supervised UDP with priors), experiments on 32 UD v1.2 treebanks, and conclusions follow a logical flow. The main contributions are stated early and supported by thorough multilingual experiments, hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is thorough. Figures/tables appear effective for comparing delexicalized vs minimally supervised results, though the omission of code availability and limited discussion of the 10,000-token training choice slightly weakens reproducibility clarity. Notation and writing are largely consistent and grammatical, with only minor places that could be tightened for readers focused on low-resource deployment details.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): This work is an incremental but useful empirical contribution rather than a fundamentally new idea: delexicalized multi-source transfer and minimally supervised/priored unsupervised parsing are established approaches, and the paper primarily contrasts them on UD v1.2 and reports that UD’s unified annotation and 10k-token concatenation suffice in this setting. The use of hand-crafted universal POS-based attach/stop priors and a limited set of rules is a pragmatic extension but not a non-trivial methodological advance, and the paper lacks a deeper theoretical account explaining why delexicalized concatenation or those priors should generalize typologically. An expert would be informed by the cross-lingual empirical findings (notably the behavior on non-Indo-European low-resource languages), but would not be surprised by the overall framing or techniques. | B(3): From my practical multilingual perspective this paper makes a useful empirical contribution—systematically comparing delexicalized multi-source transfer (MST/Malt) against an unsupervised parser augmented with simple POS-based attach/stop priors across 32 UD v1.2 treebanks—and shows important deployment-relevant tradeoffs for exotic languages. However the core ideas (delexicalized transfer, multi-source concatenation, and injection of handcrafted priors into unsupervised parsing) are largely known techniques and the priors are quite limited in scope, so the methodological novelty is moderate. The work is well-differentiated and informs experts about practical regimes where minimally supervised priors help, but it doesn’t introduce a fundamentally new modeling paradigm.

reviewer_a.justification: This work is an incremental but useful empirical contribution rather than a fundamentally new idea: delexicalized multi-source transfer and minimally supervised/priored unsupervised parsing are established approaches, and the paper primarily contrasts them on UD v1.2 and reports that UD’s unified annotation and 10k-token concatenation suffice in this setting. The use of hand-crafted universal POS-based attach/stop priors and a limited set of rules is a pragmatic extension but not a non-trivial methodological advance, and the paper lacks a deeper theoretical account explaining why delexicalized concatenation or those priors should generalize typologically. An expert would be informed by the cross-lingual empirical findings (notably the behavior on non-Indo-European low-resource languages), but would not be surprised by the overall framing or techniques.

reviewer_b.justification: From my practical multilingual perspective this paper makes a useful empirical contribution—systematically comparing delexicalized multi-source transfer (MST/Malt) against an unsupervised parser augmented with simple POS-based attach/stop priors across 32 UD v1.2 treebanks—and shows important deployment-relevant tradeoffs for exotic languages. However the core ideas (delexicalized transfer, multi-source concatenation, and injection of handcrafted priors into unsupervised parsing) are largely known techniques and the priors are quite limited in scope, so the methodological novelty is moderate. The work is well-differentiated and informs experts about practical regimes where minimally supervised priors help, but it doesn’t introduce a fundamentally new modeling paradigm.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important practical problem and provides a broad, actionable 32‑language evaluation that will inform practitioners. I give a slightly above‑average impact score because the empirical findings are useful and likely to influence deployment and follow‑up work, while acknowledging Reviewer A’s valid concerns about ad hoc priors, unexplained data truncation, and limited statistical reporting that moderate its potential for broader theoretical influence.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—cross-lingual parsing for low-resource languages—and provides a useful empirical comparison across 32 UD v1.2 treebanks showing delexicalized multi-source transfer generally outperforming a minimally supervised UDP augmented with handcrafted POS-based priors, while the latter helps many non‑Indo‑European languages. However, from a theory-first perspective its impact is moderated by reliance on ad hoc priors without sensitivity analysis, the unexplained truncation to the first 10k tokens (a potential confound), and an apparent lack of statistical significance/variance reporting. These limitations keep the contribution in the moderate-impact range: informative and likely to be cited by practitioners, but unlikely to fundamentally shift theoretical approaches to cross-lingual parsing without stronger formal or statistical support.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important, practical problem—cross‑lingual parsing for low‑resource and typologically diverse languages—and backs claims with a broad 32‑language UD evaluation, which I value highly for real‑world applicability. The finding that delexicalized multi‑source transfer benefits from UD’s unified annotation and that a lightweight, minimally supervised parser with simple POS‑based priors can outperform transfer on many non‑Indo‑European languages is actionable for practitioners. While not radically novel, the work is likely to influence deployment choices and spur follow‑ups that refine small, language‑agnostic priors and evaluate data‑efficiency tradeoffs across more exotic languages.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is a strong fit for the venue, addresses an important cross-lingual/low-resource parsing problem across many languages, and uses appropriate baselines and experimental setup. I give a 4 because practical gaps—hand‑crafted POS priors with limited sensitivity analysis, arbitrary 10k token truncation, and no statement on code/reproducibility—reduce confidence in the generality and impact; these are addressable but warrant revision before a top‑tier accept.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper sits squarely in the core NLP/ML remit of cross-lingual and low-resource parsing: it evaluates delexicalized multi-source transfer and minimally supervised priors across 32 UD v1.2 languages and compares to established baselines (McDonald et al., Rosa). The contribution will interest the venue audience, but I have scope concerns grounded in the summary: the reliance on handcrafted universal POS-based priors with limited analysis, the arbitrary truncation to the first 10k tokens per language, and the absence of mentioned code/reproducibility weaken its broad impact. With stronger justification/analysis of those methodological choices (sensitivity to priors, statistical variance reporting) this would be an excellent fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP venue: it tackles cross-lingual dependency parsing with a pragmatic, deployment-minded comparison of delexicalized multi-source transfer and a minimally supervised unsupervised parser across 32 UD languages, including many non‑Indo-European low-resource cases that I value. The experimental setup and baselines (MST/Malt, comparisons to McDonald et al. and Rosa) and reporting of hyperparameters meet community standards, and the findings about which approach suits exotic languages are directly applicable. Minor practical gaps (limited, manually specified priors, choice to truncate training to the first 10k tokens, and no mention of released code) do not undermine its relevance for this venue.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical evaluation is broad and supports the main claims across many treebanks, and that related work and limitations are well discussed. However, they also both note missing sensitivity analyses/ablations (especially for the handcrafted priors and the 10k‑token truncation) and limited reproducibility detail, which meaningfully lowers the methodological rigor. Given these substantive gaps, a conservative score of 3 reflects solid empirical breadth but insufficient analysis to merit a higher rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs a reasonably broad empirical comparison across 32 UD treebanks and reports per-parser averages and baselines, so the experiments are adequate to support the high-level claims. However, from the summary important analyses are missing: I see no statistical significance testing or variance estimates across random seeds/languages, no systematic sensitivity study or ablation of the handcrafted priors (or alternatives), and the decision to truncate each source to the first 10k tokens is neither theoretically justified nor empirically evaluated as a potential confound. Related work is treated thoroughly and limitations are acknowledged, but the work would need the above additional analyses to meet a higher standard of theoretical and empirical rigor.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental evaluation is strong in breadth — 32 UD v1.2 treebanks and direct comparisons to prior delexicalized transfer work — and supports the main claims about delexicalized MST outperforming the minimally supervised parser on average while the latter helps many non‑Indo‑European languages. Ablations/sensitivity are partially addressed (e.g. choice of 10k-token subsampling and reporting hyperparameters, and description of hand-crafted priors and λattach settings), but the treatment of priors is narrow and there is no mention of released code, which limits full reproducibility. Related work and limitations are discussed, but I would have liked more systematic sensitivity analyses for the priors and the training‑size decision to fully justify practical deployment choices in low‑resource settings.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical results are broadly supported by a reasonable evaluation across 32 treebanks and that the main conclusions (delexicalized MST wins on average; minimally supervised helps many non‑Indo‑European languages) are credible. However, both note recurring methodological gaps—no statistical significance or variance reporting, an unexplained 10k token truncation, limited/manual priors without sensitivity analysis, and no reproducibility statement—that weaken the technical validity and prevent full confidence in the claims. Given these substantive but fixable issues, a cautious mid‑level score of 3 is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents clear empirical claims and comparative experiments across 32 UD v1.2 treebanks showing delexicalized MST transfer outperforming a minimally supervised UDP variant on average and that the UDP variant helps many non-Indo-European languages; these claims are supported by reported averaged attachment scores. However, from the provided summary there is no theoretical analysis explaining why delexicalized concatenation across typologically diverse languages should work, no statistical significance tests or variance estimates across random seeds or per-language confidence intervals, and the choice to truncate to the first 10k tokens per language is not justified or ablated. The use of a small set of handcrafted POS-based priors without sensitivity analysis or learned alternatives is another unsupported assumption that weakens causal interpretation of the minimally supervised gains, so while the experiments are informative the methodological gaps prevent a fully convincing technical validation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's empirical claims are generally well supported: a multi-source delexicalized MST transfer parser was evaluated across 32 UD v1.2 treebanks and shown to outperform the minimally supervised (prior-augmented UDP) system on average, while the minimally supervised approach does better on many non-Indo-European / low-resource languages. Experimental methodology is mostly sound (proper delexicalized baselines, multi-source leave-one-out, and comparison to prior work), but there are notable practical concerns that prevent a top score: the arbitrary choice to truncate training to the first 10,000 tokens per language may bias transfer results, the manual priors are very limited in scope, no code/reproducibility statement is reported, and I see no mention of statistical significance testing. These are minor-to-moderate issues that do not overturn the main conclusions but warrant clearer justification and additional analysis.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper compares to appropriate, relevant baselines on the same dataset/metrics and contextualizes results well, including discussion of when delexicalized transfer outperforms the proposed method. I give a 4 because, while comparisons are comprehensive and pragmatic, the authors did not report variance/significance testing or thoroughly analyze the impact of truncating to the first 10,000 tokens per language, reducing confidence in the robustness of the comparative claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to reasonable and relevant baselines (delexicalized Malt and MST, and results from McDonald et al. 2011 and Rosa 2015) and uses the same UD v1.2 data and averaged UAS metric, so most comparisons are appropriate. However, the authors’ choice to truncate to the first 10,000 tokens per language and the lack of reported variance/significance testing weaken claims of fairness and robustness; these experimental-control issues are not adequately analyzed. The manuscript does acknowledge when prior methods outperform or match parts of their approach and discusses related work thoroughly, but it would benefit from statistical tests and sensitivity analyses to fully justify the comparative claims.

reviewer_b.justification: Comprehensive and pragmatic comparisons: the paper evaluates both delexicalized multi-source transfer (MST and Malt) and a minimally supervised UDP variant across 32 UD languages and reports averaged UAS, directly comparing to McDonald et al. (2011) and Rosa (2015) on comparable sets. The authors use the same dataset/metrics, explicitly acknowledge when delexicalized transfer outperforms their minimally supervised method overall while showing advantages on non‑Indo-European/exotic languages, and analyze the tradeoffs and data‑budget choices. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the baselines chosen are appropriate and recent for this line of work, so comparisons appear fair and well contextualized.

REPLICABILITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides many practical details (hyperparameters, delexicalization, datasets, and evaluation metric) but lacks released code and gives only high-level descriptions of the handcrafted priors and some preprocessing choices (10k-token cap) without justification or variance/statistical testing. These omissions mean faithful reproduction would require nontrivial implementation choices and assumptions, so the work is only partially reproducible from the paper alone.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports hyperparameters and describes key model configurations (delexicalization, MST/Malt training, UDP with injected priors) and uses publicly available UD v1.2 treebanks, so many core components are specified. However, important reproducibility gaps remain: the preprocessing choice to truncate to the first 10k tokens is neither theoretically justified nor empirically analyzed, the exact handcrafted priors are only sketchily described ("a small set" of four rules and pextstop entries) and there is no code release, making faithful reimplementation error-prone. Evaluation metrics (averaged UAS) are reported but there is no mention of statistical significance or variance estimates across seeds/languages. Given these omissions, the study is only partially reproducible from the paper alone.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper gives most practical details needed: hyperparameters are reported, the delexicalization preprocessing (replace forms/lemmas/features/relations with underscores) and the 10k-token per-language training cap are specified, and the experiments use publicly available UD v1.2 treebanks with unlabeled attachment accuracy clearly reported. However, code is not provided and the minimally supervised method relies on a small set of manually specified priors (only described at a high level — “four attachment rules” and pextstop entries with λattach defaults), so exact prior values/injection mechanics and any implementation refinements are under-specified. From an engineering standpoint this is mostly reproducible but would require some careful reimplementation choices for the priors and UDP integration.

main_claims: Compared delexicalized multi-source transfer parsing and a minimally supervised (unsupervised parser enriched with external attach/stop priors) approach across 32 languages from Universal Dependencies v1.2.

Universal Dependencies' unified annotation improves delexicalized transfer parser performance compared to earlier heterogeneous treebanks (they report higher average scores than McDonald et al. 2011 and Rosa 2015 on comparable sets).

The MST delexicalized transfer parser achieves better average performance overall, while the minimally supervised parser (with handcrafted universal POS-based priors) performs better on many non-Indo-European / low-resourced languages and is therefore suitable for exotic low-resource languages.
Using limited per-language training data (first 10,000 tokens) concatenated across languages is sufficient to train delexicalized transfer parsers in this setup.
_summary.methodology: They delexicalize UD v1.2 treebanks (forms/lemmas/features/relations replaced by underscores), use multi-source leave-one-out transfer by concatenating other languages (10,000 tokens per language) and train Malt and MST delexicalized parsers; and they run an unsupervised dependency parser (UDP) augmented with manually assigned or learned external pextstop and pextattach priors (injected into the UDP inference).
datasets: Universal Dependencies treebank collection v1.2 (32 treebanks, one per language)
metrics: attachment score / averaged accuracy (averaged unlabeled attachment accuracy is reported)
limitations: Average attachment score of the minimally supervised parser is slightly lower than the delexicalized transfer parser overall.
They limit training data to first 10,000 tokens per language to reduce training time (choice may affect results).
Only a small set of manual priors was used (four attachment rules and pextstop entries), and λattach was set to 0 for all other edge types (limited scope of priors).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - cross-lingual dependency parsing / unsupervised & delexicalized parsing

reviewer_a.name: Theory-First Linguisticist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in computational linguistics with a track record of formal models of syntactic transfer and theoretical guarantees for parsing algorithms.
traits: Penalizes papers that rely primarily on empirical comparisons without providing a clear theoretical motivation or formal analysis (e.g., no explanation of why delexicalized concatenation should be expected to work across typologically diverse languages).
Requires statistically rigorous evaluation: subtracts points if significance testing and variance estimates (e.g., per-language confidence intervals or seed variability) are missing for key claims.
Penalizes use of handcrafted priors without systematic justification or analysis of sensitivity (e.g., no ablation showing robustness to different priors or learned alternatives).

Values clarity on experimental controls: deducts points when data preprocessing choices (such as truncating to first 10k tokens) are not theoretically or empirically justified and when potential confounds (e.g., language relatedness effects) are not controlled.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on theoretical grounding and experimental rigor, privileging explanations, formal analyses, and controlled ablations over solely empirical gains.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Multilingual Engineer
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist with extensive experience building multilingual NLP systems and deploying low-resource parsing solutions in real-world pipelines.
traits: Rewards extensive multilingual empirical evaluation, especially when many low-resource and non-Indo-European languages are included (adds points for breadth of languages and realistic low-resource setups).

Favors practical, minimally supervised methods and simple, reproducible engineering solutions (gives credit for methods that are lightweight, use minimal language-specific resources, or rely on small handcrafted priors when they improve rare-language performance).

Values demonstrated applicability: rewards papers that show clear trade-offs and use-cases (e.g., delexicalized transfer better overall but minimally supervised methods better for exotic languages), and deducts less for absence of formal proofs if empirical evidence is thorough.
Penalizes papers that omit comparisons to strong, practical baselines or that fail to report runtime/complexity and data-efficiency (expects at least basic cost/efficiency reporting).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and application-focused: emphasizes broad multilingual experiments, reproducibility, and methods that are simple and useful for low-resource deployment even without formal guarantees.
